coredata.dc:title: Recovery of technical Li2CO3 from dust obtained after pyrometallurgical processing of Li-ion battery masses on a quarter-technological scale
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sobianowska-Turek, Agnieszka
Fornalczyk, Agnieszka
Zygmunt, Michał
Janosz, Michał
coredata.dc:description: The increasing use of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) necessitates the development of efficient recycling methods to manage the resulting waste stream, which includes various components such as electrode materials, electrolytes, plastics and steel. This paper examines innovative recycling processes, with a focus on hydrometallurgical techniques initiated by high-temperature reductive combustion of Li-ion masses. This thermal pretreatment transforms key chemical compounds contained in the battery mass into three products: alloy, slag and dust. The paper presents experimental results indicating that dust collected in bag filters during pyrometallurgical processing of LIBs contains significant lithium and fluorine content. By optimizing acid leaching parameters, selective lithium recovery is possible in the form of technical grade Li2CO3 with a purity of 99%. These studies indicate a promising path to increase lithium recovery efficiency while maintaining the cost-effectiveness of individual recycling operations. Overall, the research highlights the critical role of adaptive recycling strategies in addressing the challenges posed by evolving battery technologies and waste management. Interestingly, the research also addresses the potential transition to non-lithium battery technologies, such as sodium-ion and zinc-ion cells, which could significantly change the recycling landscape and reduce the need for lithium recovery from lithium-ion batteries waste.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Poland institutions: Core Hybrid journals: Wroclaw University of Science and Technology RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spent LIB
Pyrometallurgical process
Dust
Selective leaching
Recovery technical Li2CO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Qualitative and quantitative chemical composition of dust after pyrometallurgical processing of LIBs battery masses in an electric arc-resistance furnace.
Unit processes of processing battery mass derived from lithium-ion cells (LIBs) in order to obtain a commercial technical product Li2CO3 together with equipment and devices used in research.

Mass balance of the content of two basic components, lithium and fluorine, after a single acid leaching process on a quarter-technological scale of dust obtained from the pyrometallurgical processing of LIB battery masses.
The results and the basic parameters of chamber-membrane pressure filtration of the suspension formed after acid leaching of dust obtained from the pyrometallurgical processing of LIB battery masses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of quantitative and qualitative chemical analyses of lithium sediments, filtrates and washings for individual Li2CO3 technical recovery tests using 12.5% and 20.0% stoichiometric excess of Na2CO3 as the precipitation agent.

head.ce:title: Recovery of technical Li2CO3 from dust obtained after pyrometallurgical processing of Li-ion battery masses on a quarter-technological scale

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing use of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) necessitates the development of efficient recycling methods to manage the resulting waste stream, which includes various components such as electrode materials, electrolytes, plastics and steel. This paper examines innovative recycling processes, with a focus on hydrometallurgical techniques initiated by high-temperature reductive combustion of Li-ion masses. This thermal pretreatment transforms key chemical compounds contained in the battery mass into three products: alloy, slag and dust. The paper presents experimental results indicating that dust collected in bag filters during pyrometallurgical processing of LIBs contains significant lithium and fluorine content. By optimizing acid leaching parameters, selective lithium recovery is possible in the form of technical grade Li2CO3 with a purity of 99%. These studies indicate a promising path to increase lithium recovery efficiency while maintaining the cost-effectiveness of individual recycling operations. Overall, the research highlights the critical role of adaptive recycling strategies in addressing the challenges posed by evolving battery technologies and waste management. Interestingly, the research also addresses the potential transition to non-lithium battery technologies, such as sodium-ion and zinc-ion cells, which could significantly change the recycling landscape and reduce the need for lithium recovery from lithium-ion batteries waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in electric vehicles (EVs), portable electronics, and renewable energy storage has raised significant concerns regarding resource sustainability and waste management. Recognizing the need for a circular economy approach in battery production and disposal, the European Union (EU) has introduced stringent regulatory requirements to ensure the effective recovery of critical materials, including lithium.

One of the key legislative frameworks guiding LIB recycling in the EU is the Battery Regulation (EU) 2023/1542, which replaces the previous Battery Directive (2006/66/EC). This regulation mandates that battery recycling processes achieve minimum recovery efficiencies, with specific targets set for individual metals. According to Annex XII of the regulation, lithium recovery from waste batteries must reach at least 50 % by the year 2027 and 80 % by 2031, emphasizing the necessity of developing efficient and scalable recycling processes (European Association for Storage of Energy, 2025; EUR-Lex, 2025). These stringent efficiency criteria push the industry toward more advanced recovery methods that maximize material yield while minimizing environmental impact.

In the context of metal recovery from lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), the application of pyrometallurgical methods may serve as a valuable alternative to the hydrometallurgical processes currently dominating the industry, particularly for metals such as cobalt, nickel, and lithium. The use of these technologies enables the preliminary concentration of metals in alloys or slag, facilitating their separation and subsequent recovery. However, in the case of lithium, a critically important element for the economy, its efficient recovery remains challenging due to its high volatility at elevated temperatures, which leads to partial loss in the form of dust and slag (European Commission, 2025). Therefore, it is essential to develop efficient end-processing strategies that allow for lithium extraction from by-products and its reintegration into the supply chain.

The rapidly developing branch of the recycling industry dedicated to used lithium-ion cells (Li-ion, LIB), that has grown as a result of the need to manage a new and dynamically changing waste stream, mainly battery masses resulting from mechanical processing of LIBs (Ali et al., 2022; Latini et al., 2022), but also electrolytes (Yu et al., 2024; Sobianowska-Turek et al., 2023), plastics and steel housing elements (Zhang et al., 2021); has led recyclers around the world to attempt to develop new flexible techniques for their processing (Łoś et al., 2024; Harper et al., 2019). Currently, hydrometallurgical processes dominate, often preceded by reductive roasting at temperatures between 150 – 550 °C to enhance metal solubility in subsequent acid leaching (Huang et al., 2022; Leszczyńska-Sejda et al., 2024, Shoaei and Firoozi, 2025).

Although hydrometallurgical methods offer high recovery selectivity, their application involves significant chemical reagent consumption and liquid waste generation (Balachandran et al., 2021). An alternative approach is pyrometallurgical processing, which enables the preliminary concentration of metals in alloy or slag (Makuza et al., 2021; Reinhart et al., 2023). Despite numerous reviews on hydrometallurgical methods, pyrometallurgy remains underrepresented in literature (Guoxing et al., 2016; Jung et al., 2021; Cornelio et al., 2024).

An intermediate solution to the situation may be the pyrometallurgical processing of lithium-ion battery masses (Makuza et al., 2021; Reinhart et al., 2023). The remelting of LIB battery masses in high-temperature processes from 1400 °C to 1700 °C, using reducing substances: graphite, activated carbon, aluminium foils, NaHSO4, CaCl2 or NH4Cl and slag modifiers: Al2CO3, CaO, MgO and SiO2, leads to obtaining three main products: Ni-Fe-Co-Cu alloy; slag containing Al, Mn and Li; dust containing lithium (Guoxing et al., 2016; Jung et al., 2021; Cornelio et al., 2024). The main product in the form of an alloy will always be in market demand, as it serves as a precursor to materials used for example in telescopes, ring laser gyroscopes, optical and laser devices or laser benches, while from slag and dust it is possible to recover Li2CO3 of technical purity under industrial conditions and send it to companies specializing in its purification to battery purity, in which it will be suitable for use in production of LFP cells containing lithium, replacing NMC cells from the market (Xu et al., 2021; Choe et al., 2024).

Pyrometallurgical recycling of entire lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), similar to the aforementioned process, involves high-temperature smelting (around 1500 °C) to recover valuable metal alloys (Dobó et al., 2023). Thermal treatment methods, such as pyrolysis and incineration, are often used to separate cathode materials from other battery components, improving handling and processing efficiency (Yu et al., 2021). Although this method is widely applied due to its reliability in large-scale applications (Kwon and Sohn, 2020), it presents challenges related to greenhouse gas emissions, requiring further process optimization. Studies on thermodynamics and kinetics (Kwon and Sohn, 2020) and thermal behaviour analysis of black mass (Babanejad et al., 2022) contribute to the development of more sustainable pyrometallurgical recycling techniques. Leading industry players, such as ERAMET (France), UMICORE (Belgium), and Glencore (Switzerland), operate large-scale facilities, recovering Fe-Ni, Fe-Mn, Ni–Co alloys, and other valuable compounds (Dalini et al., 2020; Milian et al., 2024). The review of thermal methods, both at the industrial and laboratory scale, employed for the processing of LIBs is presented in Table 1 .

In summary, the aforementioned pyrometallurgical processes range from the description of methods for lithium recovery from alloys or slags to the pyrometallurgical treatment of LIBs. However, there is a complete lack of literature reports on such tests for dust generated during the pyrometallurgical processing. The unique aspect of this study is not only the processing of dust from the pyrometallurgical treatment but also the ability of this process to achieve a selective separation of fluorine from lithium. This distinction is particularly significant, as the presence of fluorine in battery waste poses challenges in subsequent recovery stages, and its effective removal enhances the efficiency and purity of lithium extraction.

The next step following the pyrometallurgical stage involves the recovery of valuable metals, including lithium, from the resulting products. This recovery is carried out using hydrometallurgical methods.

The second well-established approach in battery recycling is the hydrometallurgical route. This process primarily consists of leaching cathodic material derived from either the dismantling and separation stage or a prior pyrometallurgical treatment, leading to the formation of a pregnant leach solution (Zhang et al., 2025). Subsequent purification is achieved through various techniques, including chemical precipitation, electrochemical methods or solvent extraction, enabling the recovery of elements such as Li, Co, and Mn, among others (Milian et al., 2024; Islam et al., 2025, Liu et al., 2025).

The input material consisted of dust from filter bags obtained after pyrometallurgical processing of battery masses originating from the processing of used and waste lithium-ion cells (Li-ion, LIBs) of various types and kinds in an electric arc-resistance furnace, with the quantitative and qualitative chemical composition presented in Table 2 and a low bulk density of 0.25 kg/L. Pyrometallurgical tests of Li-ion battery masses were carried out on a semi-industrial scale using 537.5 kg of granulate with an average moisture content of 12.4 % containing 470.8 kg of black battery mass, in order to obtain a commercial product in the form of a Ni-Co-Cu-(Fe) alloy. The scope of work presented in Table 3 included 3 technological stages: (i) granulation of battery mass with the addition of water in a plate granulator to obtain granules of 1 mm − 15 mm in size; (ii) reductive melting of the granulated material in an electric arc-resistance furnace to obtain a Co-Ni-Cu-(Fe) alloy and manganese dust and slag with simultaneous burning of excess carbon with oxygen; (iii) Co-Ni-Cu-(Fe) alloy granulation in water. The second stage of the thermal process, i.e. melting of the lithium-ion battery mass granulate, was carried out in an 80 kW electric arc-resistance furnace with usable capacity of 50 kg, equipped with magnesite lining Normag M92, manufactured by Ropczyce Magnesite Plant S.A.. The furnace was equipped with a metal tapping hole at the level of its hearth and a chute for tapping slag by tilting the furnace, a lifting vault cooled by water, two graphite electrodes with a diameter of 38 mm, a charging hole in the vault and a gas exhaust. The process gases were cooled in a cyclone cooler and de-dusted in a bag filter. The research material was characterized by a high lithium concentration of 10.0 wt%, higher than in the LIB battery masses obtained after mechanical processing of used and/or waste cells, but also a higher fluorine content of 9.50 wt% (Urbańska, 2020; Leszczyńska-Sejda et al., 2024).

The recovery of technical Li2CO3 from dust after pyrometallurgical processing of battery masses in an electric arc-resistance furnace was carried out on a quarter-technological scale using 42.5 kg of research material. The main objective of the experimental work was to obtain salt with a minimum content of 99.0 % Li2CO3 and impurities not greater than: 0.01 % − 0.015 % Cl, 0.0003 % − 0.001 % K, 0.06 % − 0.08 % Na, 0.004 % − 0.01 % Mg, 0.04 % − 0.05 % SO4 2-, 0.001 % − 0.003 % Fe2O3 and 0.01 % − 0.75 % Ca (Rockwood Lithium Inc., 2024). The tests included four technological stages: acid leaching, phase separation of the resulting suspension, recovery of technological lithium carbonate and purification of the Li2CO3 product to technical purity.

The acid leaching procedure was developed in earlier research works of the R&D Team Elemental Strategic Metals Sp. z o.o. and guaranteed the lithium leaching yield above 50 % and fluorine concentration < 1.0 % in the solution. Acid and alkaline leaching experiments on laboratory scale were carried out using H2SO4, HCl and NaOH. The concentration of leaching agents for 0,500 kg of dust samples was 259 g/L − H2SO4, 108 g/L − HCl and 196 g/L − NaOH, respectively. Leaching tests were carried out for a solid to liquid phase ratio of 1/10, temperature 50 °C and time 2 h. Due to the absorbed selectivity of lithium leaching with a small degree of transfer to the fluorine solution, the laboratory work was continued only for H2SO4 as the leaching agent. The obtained results of preliminary studies allowed us to state that it is possible to selectively leach lithium with respect to fluorine under the conditions presented below for a quarter-technological scale. It should be noted that there are no available research results in the literature covering the processes of recovery of raw materials from dust after thermal processing of used LIBs. Only three research works presenting the recovery of lithium Li2CO3 from ash and slags are published (Verhaeghe et al. 2011; Brückner et al., 2020; Xu and Sun, 2021).

The acid leaching of dust was carried out in a 0.4 m3 reactor (working volume of 0.3 m3) equipped with a classic mixer using the following parameters: amount of research material 42.5 kg, solid to liquid phase ratio (s/l) – 1/4, H2SO4 solution with a concentration of 137.2 g/L, leaching time 2 h, process temperature 50 °C. The acid leaching of the research material was carried out according to the following procedure: the dust was dosed to the reactor with H2SO4 solution for 30 min, the initial process temperature was at the level of 20 °C, at the completion of dust dosing temperature increased to 45 °C, further the process was carried out at a constant temperature of 50 °C for the next 2 h with recording of the process parameters while monitoring the exhaust gases.

After the completion of dust leaching, the resulting suspension was subjected to pressure filtration using a chamber-membrane press. The concentration of lithium ions in the obtained solution was determined with the use of the ICP-OES method while fluorine concentration was determined with the use of the ion-selective electrode method, same methods were used for sediment after microwave mineralization of the solids.

Phase separation of the resulting suspension was carried out using a chamber-membrane pressure press (CM press) with vertically arranged plates, with plate dimensions of 630 mm x 630 mm and a chamber thickness of 35 mm in two options: two filter chambers with a total chamber volume of 12.9 L (2 x 6.45 L) and four filter chambers with a total chamber volume of 25.8 L (4 x 6.45 L), the obtained solution was redirected to the Li2CO3 recovery stage.

The recovery of technical lithium carbonate by precipitation method was carried out using 5 L of solution obtained after acidic leaching of the research material to which Na2CO3 was added in the form of a solution with a concentration of 280 g/L with 12.5 % or 20.0 % stoichiometric excess. The reaction was carried out at 95 °C for 2.5 h (of which 0.5 h was after the end of dosing), the Na2CO3 solution dosing rate was ∼ 0.015 L/min, the stirring speed 200 rpm. The obtained Li2CO3 precipitate was separated from the solution using vacuum filtration. The precipitate on the Büchner funnel was washed with hot water and ethanol. In the obtained sediment, dried at 90 °C, the concentrations of Li, Na, K, Mg, Ca, Fe, Al, ions were determined using the ICP-OES method while SO4 2- and Cl- were determined using ion chromatography (IEC), preceded by microwave mineralization of the solid sediment sample.

Purification of the Li2CO3 product to technical purity was carried out using 0.050 kg sample of the isolated lithium product. Solid Li2CO3 was suspended in demineralized water in 1:30 ratio. The suspension was saturated with gaseous CO2 with a CO2 flow of 3 L/min for 2 h in room temperature. After 2 h, the undissolved residue was separated from the solution using vacuum filtration on a Büchner funnel. The filtrate was heated to 90 °C and stirred for another 1 h, after which the crystallized Li2CO3 precipitate was separated from the solution while hot, using a Büchner funnel. Afterwards, a new portion of crude Li2CO3 was added to the remaining filtrate and the whole procedure was repeated three times. The contents of Li, Na, K, Mg, Ca, Fe, Al, ions in the obtained solutions, precipitates and Li2CO3 crystals were determined using the ICP-OES method while SO4 2- and Cl- were determined using ion chromatography (IEC), preceded by microwave mineralization of samples of solid precipitates and crystals.

All experimental works on a quarter-technological scale, including quantitative and qualitative analyses of solutions, solid samples and waste gases, were commissioned and performed in accordance with the research concept and under the substantive supervision of the R&D Team of Elemental Strategic Metals Sp. z o.o. in the Łukasiewicz Research Network – Institute of Non-Ferrous Metals in Gliwice (2024) which has an industrial research infrastructure.

Below, the research results are presented along with a discussion for individual technological stages of technical Li2CO3 recovery from dust obtained after pyrometallurgical processing of Li-ion battery masses.

As a result of a single acid leaching process of the research material, 160 L of a solution with a neutral pH (pH ∼ 7.0) was obtained, in which the concentration of lithium ions was 15.8 g/L with a simultaneous very low concentration of fluoride ions at the level of 0.20 g/L, giving the appropriate leaching degrees of these elements at the level of about 60 % for Li and 0.80 % for F. The total degree of mass loss was 36.9 %, while the concentrations of lithium and fluorine in the sediment after phase separation of the resulting suspension were 7.92 % for Li and 16.50 % for F. The mass balance of the content of two basic components lithium and fluorine after a single acid leaching process on a quarter-technological scale of dust obtained from the pyrometallurgical processing of battery masses, presented in Table 4 , shows a very high compliance for both fluorine and lithium, despite the observed inhomogeneity of the charge, which indicates the possibility of problem-free implementation of the method in industrial conditions. Additionally, the obtained results for the determination of gaseous pollutants formed in the dust leaching reaction indicate that a small part of the charge material was entrained during dosing to the reactor, which resulted in the absorption of the tested material in the solutions trap of the applied measuring systems. It is worth noting, however, that the determination of the concentration of individual substances in the exhaust gases, especially metals and HF, constitutes a small amount of the initial charge mass of the leached dusts. An important observed aspect is lack of hydrogen release during the reaction. This allows us to assume that in the case of gases formed during the tested process, there is no risk of their explosion from above the reactor. The calculated released mass of individual pollutants is at the level of several hundred milligrams and is a small part of the input amount of leached material, i.e. 42.5 kg. The only exception is CO2, for which the calculated released mass was approximately 7.0 kg. All obtained results of determining the content of H2SO4 mist were below the detection limit of the applied method. During the tests, no presence of SO2, CO or TVOC was detected in the gases. The measurements only showed that CO2 and NOx were released from the reaction system at the start of the reaction, after which, after 30 min, the emission ceased.

The first approach to filtration indicated the need to remove pieces of material from the suspension, which prevented proper fed to the filter press − blockage of the pump feeding the filter press. For this reason, the entire suspension from the reactor was sieved through a sieve ø equal to 2.5 mm and collected in a buffer tank, from which the pressure filtration process was then carried out. Removal of the fraction ø > 2.5 mm enabled the continuation of the suspension filtration process using a chamber-membrane press. The total mass of the fraction ø > 2.5 mm was 1.32 kg of wet mass. The results of the pressure filtration tests of the suspension, from which the interfering fraction was removed, with two different numbers of filter chambers: F1 − two filter chambers, 35 mm − 35 mm plates, volume 12.9 L (2 x 6.45 L) and F2 − four filter chambers, 35 mm − 35 mm − 35 mm − 35 mm, volume 25.8 L (4 x 6.45 L) together with detailed process parameters from both filtrations are given in Table 5 . The sediment after filtration had a lumpy structure and easily fell off the surface of the filter fabrics, appropriate for the future industrial process.

As a result of experiments conducted using 20 % stoichiometric excess of Na2CO3 at a concentration of 280 g/L, 0.347 kg Li2CO3 was obtained together with 7.0 L of filtrate and 0.75 L of washings. The efficiency of lithium precipitation in the form of carbonate salts was 86.5 %, which is a value close to the efficiency obtained using 12.5 % excess. This indicates that a larger amount of excess Na2CO3 does not affect the efficiency of lithium precipitation from the solution after acid leaching of the research material. The lithium concentration in the sediments in both samples was high and reached 17.3 % for 12.5 % Na2CO3 excess and 19.0 % for 20.0 % Na2CO3 excess. The results of quantitative and qualitative chemical analyses of lithium sediments, filtrates and washings for individual technical Li2CO3 recovery tests are presented in Table 6 and Fig. 1 .

Attempts to purify Li2CO3 were performed with 0.050 kg of precipitate obtained from the test with 20 % stoichiometric excess of Na2CO3. After the purification of raw Li2CO3 (0 cycle, I cycle, II cycle and III cycle), 0.02298 kg, 0.04215 kg, 0.04700 kg and 0.04320 kg of Li2CO3 were obtained, respectively. The results of quantitative and qualitative chemical analyses of pollutant sediments and lithium salts before and after the purification stage are presented in Tables 7 and 8
. After analysing the obtained results of the quantitative and qualitative chemical composition of Li2CO3 salts after the three-stage purification with respect to the permissible values in the composition of commercial technical Li2CO3, i.e. Cl: 0.01–0.015 wt%, Mg: 0.004–0.01 wt%, K: 0.0003–0.001 wt%, SO4 2-: 0.04–0.05 wt%, Na: 0.06–0.08 wt%, Ca: 0.01–0.016 wt% and Fe in the form of Fe2O3: 0.001–0.003 wt%. it can be clearly stated that for the final Li2CO3 product the content of chlorine was exceeded by 0.015 % by weight, magnesium by 0.018 % by weight and SO4 2- by 0.05 % by weight. Therefore, an additional purification step by CO2 saturation and recrystallization should be tested in industrial conditions and/or a single solution purification process should be planned using membrane methods, ion exchange, solvent extraction or ion flotation, which would allow for purification of Li2CO3 to the level required for a technical commercial product from the main impurities of chlorine, magnesium and sodium (Zhou et al., 2023; Milian et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2024; Nazari et al. 2024).

The experimental results presented in this paper related to the recovery of Li2CO3 product of technical purity from dust obtained after the melting of LIB battery masses allow for the formulation of the following conclusions:
•
by-products of thermal recycling processes of lithium-ion battery masses in the form of dust accumulating in filter bags contain significant amounts of lithium > 10.0 % by weight, which can be recovered by hydrometallurgical methods in the form of technical Li2CO3;
•
appropriately selected parameters of acidic dust leaching, i.e. s/l = 0.25, CH2SO4 = 137.2 g/L, t = 2h and T = 50 °C, allow for selective transfer of lithium to the solution at a very low concentration of fluoride ions, which is impossible in the classical hydrometallurgical method of LIB battery masses;
•
the process of obtaining a technical product with a minimum content of 99.0 % Li2CO3 is possible on a transmission scale, it only requires an additional unit step of purifying the solution after acid leaching from sodium and magnesium ions using commercially available physicochemical methods.

Agnieszka Sobianowska-Turek: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Agnieszka Fornalczyk: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Michał Zygmunt: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Michał Janosz: Methodology, Conceptualization.

ESM’s project has been co-financed by the European Union according to the EC decision C (2019) 8823 of December 9, 2019, within the call no. 8 / 1.1.1/2020 - Fast track – IPCEI organized by the Polish National Centre for Research and Development.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preprocessing of spent lithium-ion batteries for recycling: Need, methods, and trends
Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ali, H., Khan, H.A., Pecht, M., 2022. Preprocessing of spent lithium-ion batteries for recycling: Need, methods, and trends. Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev. 168, 112809. doi: 10.1016/j.rser.2022.112809.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-Temperature Behavior of Spent Li-Ion Battery Black Mass in Inert Atmosphere
J. Sustain. Metall.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Babanejad, S., Ahmed, H., Andersson, C., Samuelsson, C., Lennartsson, A., Hall, B., Arnerlöf, L., 2022. High-Temperature Behavior of Spent Li-Ion Battery Black Mass in Inert Atmosphere. J. Sustain. Metall. 8, 566-581. doi: 10.1007/s40831-022-00514-y.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Balachandran et al., 2021
